name: Chinese Dragon Full
description: The Chinese dragon, also known as the Long, is a powerful and benevolent creature in Chinese mythology, representing potent and auspicious powers, particularly control over water, rainfall, typhoons, and floods. Unlike the often malevolent dragons of Western lore, Chinese dragons are symbols of strength, good fortune, wisdom, and prosperity. They are often depicted with a long, serpentine body covered in scales, four legs with sharp claws, and a dragon-like head with horns, whiskers, and piercing eyes. The color of the dragon can indicate its age, power, or element it is associated with, with gold and yellow often representing the emperor or imperial power. They are revered as divine beings and are deeply ingrained in Chinese culture, art, and traditions, appearing in everything from imperial emblems to festive dances. Chinese dragons are also associated with the yang principle, representing masculinity, activity, and light. They are believed to be able to bring rain during droughts and protect against evil spirits. These dragons are intelligent and wise, often acting as advisors or guardians to those who are worthy. Their presence brings good luck and prosperity to those around them. The dragon is one of the four symbols of the Chinese constellations, along with the Phoenix, Tiger, and Tortoise.
subtitle: Symbol of power and good fortune
appearance: A serpentine creature with a long, scaled body, four legs with sharp claws, a dragon-like head with horns, whiskers, and piercing eyes. The color varies, often gold, green, blue, or red, depending on its age, power, and elemental association. It possesses a long, flowing mane and beard.
habitat: Celestial realms, bodies of water (rivers, lakes, oceans), mountains, and imperial palaces.
behavior: Generally benevolent and wise, bringing good fortune and prosperity. Can be fierce and protective when angered. Often serves as guardians or advisors.

keyMyths.title: The Dragon King
keyMyths.description: Dragon Kings rule over the four seas, controlling the weather and aquatic life. They are powerful deities who can bring both prosperity and destruction depending on their mood.

keyMyths.title: Dragon Gate
keyMyths.description: Legend says that carp that can leap over the Dragon Gate on the Yellow River will transform into dragons. This symbolizes perseverance and achieving great success.

sources.author: E.T.C. Werner
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends.

sources.author: Lihui Yang and Deming An
sources.description: A detailed exploration of Chinese mythology, including various dragon types and their symbolism.
